fragments.content: Transient ischemic attack (TIA) is a transient episode of neurological dysfunction caused by focal brain, spinal cord, or retinal ischemia without acute infarction. Traditional definition: symptoms <24 hours; tissue-based definition: no infarction on MRI DWI. TIA is a medical emergency—stroke risk is 10-15% in first 90 days, with ~50% occurring within 48 hours ('warning stroke'). Typical symptoms: sudden onset focal deficit (unilateral weakness/numbness, aphasia, dysarthria, visual field defect, ataxia); negative symptoms (loss of function) rather than positive (spreading tingling suggests migraine). Amaurosis fugax: transient monocular vision loss from ipsilateral carotid emboli ('curtain descending'). Duration typically <1 hour (median 10 minutes). ABCD2 score predicts early stroke risk: Age ≥60 (1), BP ≥140/90 (1), Clinical features (unilateral weakness 2, speech impairment without weakness 1), Duration ≥60 min (1), Diabetes (1). Score 0-3 low risk, 4-5 moderate, 6-7 high risk.

TIA treatment focuses on urgent evaluation and secondary prevention. Immediate management: aspirin 160-325mg ASAP (reduces early stroke risk by ~80%). Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT): clopidogrel 75mg daily PLUS aspirin 81mg daily for 21 days (CHANCE/POINT trials—reduces 90-day stroke risk by ~25% in high-risk TIA/minor stroke), then clopidogrel monotherapy. For atrial fibrillation: anticoagulation with DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) instead of antiplatelet; timing depends on infarct size (immediate for TIA, delayed 3-14 days for stroke to reduce hemorrhagic conversion risk). High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80mg or rosuvastatin 20-40mg) regardless of LDL. BP control target <130/80 long-term. Carotid revascularization: symptomatic carotid stenosis 70-99% benefits from carotid endarterectomy (CEA) within 2 weeks (NASCET trial); 50-69% modest benefit in men; <50% no benefit. Carotid stenting alternative for high surgical risk. Intracranial stenosis: aggressive medical management (DAPT × 90 days, intensive risk factor control) preferred over stenting (SAMMPRIS trial). Lifestyle: smoking cessation, Mediterranean diet, exercise, weight management.

TIA diagnosis is clinical but requires urgent workup to confirm etiology and guide prevention. Neuroimaging: MRI brain with DWI within 24 hours—30-50% of clinically diagnosed TIAs show acute infarction (then classified as stroke, not TIA). Vascular imaging: carotid ultrasound or CTA/MRA head and neck to identify extracranial/intracranial stenosis, dissection, occlusion. Cardiac evaluation: ECG for atrial fibrillation, telemetry/Holter monitor (prolonged monitoring increases AF detection), echocardiogram (TTE/TEE) for cardioembolic sources (thrombus, PFO, valvular disease, cardiomyopathy). Laboratory: CBC, CMP, fasting lipid panel, HbA1c, coagulation studies. Hypercoagulable testing if young (<50) or suggestive history. EEG if seizure suspected (positive symptoms, postictal deficits). Differential diagnosis: seizure/Todd's paralysis (positive spreading symptoms, post-event confusion), migraine aura (gradual spread, positive symptoms, headache), hypoglycemia, peripheral vertigo, functional disorder. Risk stratification: ABCD2 score guides urgency (high-risk scores 6-7 warrant hospital admission); however, imaging findings (DWI positivity, significant stenosis) better predict recurrence than ABCD2 alone. Rapid access TIA clinics reduce time to treatment and stroke risk.
